questions.question: A $0.4\text{ kg}$ ball is kicked from rest and reaches a speed of $5\text{ m/s}$. Calculate the impulse given to the ball.
questions.explanation:
Impulse is the change in momentum: $Delta(mv) = 0.4\text{ kg} \times (5 - 0)\text{ m/s} = 2.0\text{ kg m/s}$ (or $N s )$.

**Common mistake:** Using only the speed (5.0$) or mass ($0.4$) as the impulse$.

questions.question: A ball receives an impulse of $2.0\text{ N s}$. If the average force applied was $100\text{ N}$, how long was the contact time?
questions.explanation:
Using $FDelta t = Impulse , so \Delta t = Impulse / F = 2.0 / 100 = 0.02\text{ s}$. 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying the numbers ($200$) instead of dividing.

questions.question: A car of mass $1000\text{ kg}$ is travelling at $5\text{ m/s}$ and comes to a complete stop. What is the change in its momentum?
questions.explanation:
Change in momentum $= mv - mu = 1000 \times (0 - 5) = -5000\text{ kg m/s}$. Magnitude is $5000\text{ kg m/s}$. 

**Common mistake:** Dividing mass by velocity ($200$).

questions.question: Object X and Object Y undergo the same change in momentum. Object X stops in $0.1\text{ s}$, while Object Y stops in $0.5\text{ s}$. How do the forces acting on them compare?
options: $The force on X is 5 \times greater than on Y$
$The force on Y is 5 \times greater than on X$
The forces are equal because the impulse is the same
The force on X is half that of Y
questions.explanation:
Since $F = \Delta p / t$, if time is $1$ \times smaller ($0.1$ vs $0.5$), the force must be^5 \times larger. 

**Common mistake:** Assuming same impulse means same force regardless of time.

questions.question: Which equation correctly represents the relationship between force ($F$), mass ($m$), change in velocity ($\Delta v$), and time ($t$)?
questions.explanation:
This is derived from $F = \Delta p / \Delta t$ where $\Delta p = m \Delta v$. So $F = m \Delta v / t$. 

**Common mistake:** Placing time in the numerator or ignoring the division entirely.
